title: 73.	Minimum cost to cut a stick.
description: 73.	Minimum cost to cut a stick.

examples.explanation:
1. Sort cuts and add boundaries: [0,1,3,4,5,7]
2. Define dp(i,j) = min cost between boundaries i and j
3. dp(0,5): try cuts at positions 1,2,3,4 (indices 1-4)
   - Cut at 1: cost = 7-0=7 + dp(0,1)=0 + dp(1,5)
     dp(1,5): try cuts at positions 2,3,4
       Cut at 3: cost = 7-1=6 + dp(1,2)=0 + dp(2,5)
         dp(2,5): try cuts at positions 3,4
           Cut at 4: cost = 7-3=4 + dp(2,3)=0 + dp(3,5)
             dp(3,5): try cut at position 4
               Cut at 5: cost = 7-4=3 + dp(3,4)=0 + dp(4,5)=0 = 3
             So dp(3,5)=3
           So cut at 4 gives 4+3=7
           Cut at 5: cost = 7-3=4 + dp(2,4) + dp(4,5)=0
             dp(2,4): try cut at position 3
               Cut at 4: cost = 5-3=2 + dp(2,3)=0 + dp(3,4)=0 = 2
             So dp(2,4)=2
           So cut at 5 gives 4+2=6
         So dp(2,5)=min(7,6)=6
       So cut at 3 gives 6+6=12
       Similarly evaluate cuts at 4 and 5
     dp(1,5)=12
   So cut at 1 gives 7+12=19
   Continue evaluating cuts at 3,4,5
   Minimum found is 16
4. Return 16

1. Sort cuts: [1,2,4,5,6], add boundaries: [0,1,2,4,5,6,9]
2. dp(0,6): try cuts at positions 1-5
   - Cut at 1: cost=9-0=9 + dp(0,1)=0 + dp(1,6)
     dp(1,6): try cuts at positions 2-5
       Cut at 2: cost=9-1=8 + dp(1,2)=0 + dp(2,6)
         dp(2,6): try cuts at positions 3-5
           Cut at 4: cost=9-2=7 + dp(2,3)=0 + dp(3,6)
             dp(3,6): try cuts at positions 4,5
               Cut at 5: cost=9-4=5 + dp(3,4)=0 + dp(4,6)
                 dp(4,6): try cut at position 5
                   Cut at 6: cost=9-5=4 + dp(4,5)=0 + dp(5,6)=0 = 4
                 So dp(4,6)=4
               So cut at 5 gives 5+4=9
               Cut at 6: cost=9-4=5 + dp(3,5) + dp(5,6)=0
                 dp(3,5): try cut at position 4
                   Cut at 5: cost=6-4=2 + dp(3,4)=0 + dp(4,5)=0 = 2
                 So dp(3,5)=2
               So cut at 6 gives 5+2=7
             So dp(3,6)=min(9,7)=7
           So cut at 4 gives 7+7=14
           Continue evaluating cuts at 5,6
         dp(2,6)=14
       So cut at 2 gives 8+14=22
       Continue evaluating cuts at 4,5,6
     dp(1,6)=22
   So cut at 1 gives 9+22=31
   Continue evaluating cuts at 2,4,5,6
   Minimum found is 22
3. Return 22

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion with Memoization
approaches.summary: Try all possible cut sequences recursively, storing results in memoization table to avoid recomputation. This explores all permutations of cut orders to find minimum cost.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Cost to Cut a Stick with Memoized Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding minimum cost to cut a stick at given positions using recursive exploration with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum total cost to cut a stick of length n at specified cut positions. The cost of each cut equals the current length of the stick segment being cut. The brute force approach explores all possible sequences of cuts recursively, but this leads to exponential time complexity. Memoization dramatically improves performance by storing computed results for subproblems. The intuition is that cutting a stick between positions i and j can be broken down into: cost of current cut (j-i) plus minimum cost to cut left segment (i to cut position) plus minimum cost to cut right segment (cut position to j). We try all possible first cuts in the current segment and take the minimum. The base case occurs when there are no cuts to make in the current segment. The algorithm sorts cut positions and adds 0 and n as boundaries. The memo table dp[i][j] stores minimum cost to cut between boundary i and boundary j. Time complexity is O(m^3) where m is number of cuts plus 2 boundaries, since we have O(m^2) states and each state tries O(m) cuts. Space complexity is O(m^2) for the memo table. This approach connects to L1 by using the recursive helper function as the core computation engine. Common mistakes include not sorting cuts, not adding boundaries, incorrect base case handling, and improper memoization indexing. Real code pattern uses @lru_cache or explicit 2D array.

examples.trace:
1. Sort cuts and add boundaries: [0,1,3,4,5,7]
2. Define dp(i,j) = min cost between boundaries i and j
3. dp(0,5): try cuts at positions 1,2,3,4 (indices 1-4)
   - Cut at 1: cost = 7-0=7 + dp(0,1)=0 + dp(1,5)
     dp(1,5): try cuts at positions 2,3,4
       Cut at 3: cost = 7-1=6 + dp(1,2)=0 + dp(2,5)
         dp(2,5): try cuts at positions 3,4
           Cut at 4: cost = 7-3=4 + dp(2,3)=0 + dp(3,5)
             dp(3,5): try cut at position 4
               Cut at 5: cost = 7-4=3 + dp(3,4)=0 + dp(4,5)=0 = 3
             So dp(3,5)=3
           So cut at 4 gives 4+3=7
           Cut at 5: cost = 7-3=4 + dp(2,4) + dp(4,5)=0
             dp(2,4): try cut at position 3
               Cut at 4: cost = 5-3=2 + dp(2,3)=0 + dp(3,4)=0 = 2
             So dp(2,4)=2
           So cut at 5 gives 4+2=6
         So dp(2,5)=min(7,6)=6
       So cut at 3 gives 6+6=12
       Similarly evaluate cuts at 4 and 5
     dp(1,5)=12
   So cut at 1 gives 7+12=19
   Continue evaluating cuts at 3,4,5
   Minimum found is 16
4. Return 16

1. Sort cuts: [1,2,4,5,6], add boundaries: [0,1,2,4,5,6,9]
2. dp(0,6): try cuts at positions 1-5
   - Cut at 1: cost=9-0=9 + dp(0,1)=0 + dp(1,6)
     dp(1,6): try cuts at positions 2-5
       Cut at 2: cost=9-1=8 + dp(1,2)=0 + dp(2,6)
         dp(2,6): try cuts at positions 3-5
           Cut at 4: cost=9-2=7 + dp(2,3)=0 + dp(3,6)
             dp(3,6): try cuts at positions 4,5
               Cut at 5: cost=9-4=5 + dp(3,4)=0 + dp(4,6)
                 dp(4,6): try cut at position 5
                   Cut at 6: cost=9-5=4 + dp(4,5)=0 + dp(5,6)=0 = 4
                 So dp(4,6)=4
               So cut at 5 gives 5+4=9
               Cut at 6: cost=9-4=5 + dp(3,5) + dp(5,6)=0
                 dp(3,5): try cut at position 4
                   Cut at 5: cost=6-4=2 + dp(3,4)=0 + dp(4,5)=0 = 2
                 So dp(3,5)=2
               So cut at 6 gives 5+2=7
             So dp(3,6)=min(9,7)=7
           So cut at 4 gives 7+7=14
           Continue evaluating cuts at 5,6
         dp(2,6)=14
       So cut at 2 gives 8+14=22
       Continue evaluating cuts at 4,5,6
     dp(1,6)=22
   So cut at 1 gives 9+22=31
   Continue evaluating cuts at 2,4,5,6
   Minimum found is 22
3. Return 22

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization for Stick Cutting
ladders.desc: The recursive helper function that computes minimum cost for cutting a stick segment between two boundaries.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the solution: a recursive function that takes two boundary indices (i,j) representing a stick segment from cuts[i] to cuts[j] (where cuts includes boundaries 0 and n), and returns the minimum cost to make all cuts between these boundaries. The intuition is divide-and-conquer: to cut a segment, we must choose a first cut somewhere in between, which splits the problem into two independent subproblems. The cost is the current segment length plus the minimum costs of the left and right segments. We try every possible first cut and take the minimum. The base case occurs when j == i+1, meaning there are no cuts to make in this segment (the boundaries are adjacent in the sorted cuts array). Memoization stores results in a 2D table dp[i][j] to avoid recomputation. Time complexity for this function called on the full range is O(m^3) where m is the number of boundaries, as there are O(m^2) states and each computes over O(m) possible cuts. Space complexity is O(m^2) for the memo table. This connects to L0 as the main computational engine, and to L2 which provides the core recurrence relation. Common mistakes include incorrect base case (should return 0 when no cuts), off-by-one errors in indices, and not checking that cuts are between i and j. Real code pattern uses recursion with memoization decorator or explicit table lookup.

examples.trace:
1. cuts=[0,2,4,7], i=0, j=3
2. Base case: j=3, i=0, j>i+1 so not base
3. Initialize min_cost = INF
4. Try cut k from i+1 to j-1:
   - k=1 (cut at 2): cost = cuts[3]-cuts[0]=7 + dp(0,1) + dp(1,3)
     dp(0,1): j=1,i=0 -> j==i+1 -> return 0
     dp(1,3): try cuts k=2
       k=2 (cut at 4): cost = cuts[3]-cuts[1]=5 + dp(1,2) + dp(2,3)
         dp(1,2): j=2,i=1 -> j==i+1 -> return 0
         dp(2,3): j=3,i=2 -> j==i+1 -> return 0
       So cost = 5+0+0=5
     So dp(1,3)=5
   So cut at 2 gives 7+0+5=12
   - k=2 (cut at 4): cost = 7 + dp(0,2) + dp(2,3)
     dp(0,2): try cut k=1
       k=1 (cut at 2): cost = cuts[2]-cuts[0]=4 + dp(0,1) + dp(1,2) = 4+0+0=4
     So dp(0,2)=4
     dp(2,3)=0
   So cut at 4 gives 7+4+0=11
5. min_cost = min(12,11)=11
6. Return 11

1. cuts=[0,1,3,6], i=1, j=3
2. Not base case (j=3,i=1,j>i+1)
3. min_cost = INF
4. Try k from 2 to 2 (only k=2):
   - k=2 (cut at 3): cost = cuts[3]-cuts[1]=6-1=5 + dp(1,2) + dp(2,3)
     dp(1,2): j=2,i=1 -> j==i+1 -> return 0
     dp(2,3): j=3,i=2 -> j==i+1 -> return 0
   So cost = 5+0+0=5
5. min_cost=5
6. Return 5

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for Minimum Cutting Cost
ladders.desc: The mathematical recurrence that defines minimum cost for cutting a stick segment.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recurrence relation that drives the solution: dp[i][j] = min_{k from i+1 to j-1} (cuts[j] - cuts[i] + dp[i][k] + dp[k][j]), where cuts is a sorted array including boundaries 0 and n, and i,j are indices in this array representing a segment from cuts[i] to cuts[j]. The intuition is that to cut all cuts between positions cuts[i] and cuts[j], we must choose some cut cuts[k] as the first cut. This choice splits the segment into two independent subsegments: [cuts[i], cuts[k]] and [cuts[k], cuts[j]]. The cost of making cut k is the current segment length (cuts[j]-cuts[i]), plus the minimum costs to cut the left and right segments recursively. We minimize over all possible first cuts k. The base case is when j == i+1, meaning there are no cuts between these boundaries, so dp[i][j] = 0. This recurrence exhibits optimal substructure: the optimal solution for a segment can be constructed from optimal solutions of smaller segments. It also has overlapping subproblems, making memoization beneficial. Time complexity for computing one dp[i][j] is O(j-i-1) = O(m) where m is number of boundaries. Space for the relation itself is O(1). This connects to L1 as the formula implemented in the recursive function, and to L3 which provides the sorted cuts array. Common mistakes include incorrect segment length calculation, wrong range for k, or missing base case. Real code pattern expresses this as a loop over k within the recursive function.

examples.trace:
1. cuts=[0,2,5,9], segment length = cuts[3]-cuts[0]=9
2. Try k=1 (cut at 2): cost = 9 + dp[0][1] + dp[1][3]
   dp[0][1]: j=1,i=0 -> base case -> 0
   dp[1][3]: segment [2,9], length=7, try k=2
     k=2 (cut at 5): cost = 7 + dp[1][2] + dp[2][3]
       dp[1][2]: base -> 0
       dp[2][3]: base -> 0
     So dp[1][3]=7
   So cost = 9+0+7=16
3. Try k=2 (cut at 5): cost = 9 + dp[0][2] + dp[2][3]
   dp[0][2]: segment [0,5], length=5, try k=1
     k=1 (cut at 2): cost = 5 + dp[0][1] + dp[1][2] = 5+0+0=5
   So dp[0][2]=5
   dp[2][3]: base -> 0
   So cost = 9+5+0=14
4. Minimum is min(16,14)=14? Wait, let's recompute carefully.
   Actually for k=1: dp[1][3] we computed as 7, so 9+0+7=16
   For k=2: dp[0][2]=5, so 9+5+0=14
   So min is 14, but expected output is 13. Let's trace dp[1][3] more carefully:
   dp[1][3]: cuts[1]=2, cuts[3]=9, length=7
   Try k=2: cost=7 + dp[1][2] + dp[2][3] = 7+0+0=7
   That's correct, so dp[1][3]=7
   So 16 and 14, min=14
   But wait, maybe there's another cut? k only goes from i+1 to j-1, so k=1,2.
   Let's compute manually: cuts at 2 and 5.
   If cut at 2 first: cost=9 (stick length 9) + cost to cut [2,9] which has cut at 5: cost=7 -> total 16
   If cut at 5 first: cost=9 + cost to cut [0,5] which has cut at 2: cost=5 -> total 14
   So minimum is 14, not 13. My earlier expected output was wrong. Correct output is 14.
   So for this example, output should be 14.
5. Therefore dp[0][3]=14

1. cuts=[0,1,4,6], but i=0,j=2 means segment [0,4], length=4
2. Only k=1 (cut at 1) possible
3. cost = cuts[2]-cuts[0]=4 + dp[0][1] + dp[1][2]
   dp[0][1]: base -> 0
   dp[1][2]: base -> 0
4. So cost = 4+0+0=4
5. dp[0][2]=4

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting and Preparing Cut Positions with Boundaries
ladders.desc: Preprocessing step: sort cut positions and add boundary positions 0 and n.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves preparing the input data for the recurrence. Given a stick length n and a list of cut positions, we first sort the cuts in ascending order. Then, we create a new array that includes 0 (left boundary), all sorted cuts, and n (right boundary). This preprocessing is crucial because: (1) Sorting ensures cuts are in increasing order, which simplifies the recurrence as we only consider segments between consecutive boundaries. (2) Adding boundaries 0 and n provides fixed endpoints for the entire stick, allowing the recurrence to work on segments defined by indices in this array. The intuition is that cutting happens between these boundary positions, and the cost depends on the distance between boundaries. Without sorting, we couldn't guarantee that cuts between i and j are actually within that segment. Without boundaries, we'd have to handle edge cases separately. The algorithm is straightforward: create list boundaries = [0] + sorted(cuts) + [n]. Time complexity is O(c log c) for sorting, where c is number of cuts. Space complexity is O(c) for the new list. This connects to L2 by providing the 'cuts' array used in the recurrence, and to L4 which involves basic array operations. Common mistakes include not sorting, adding boundaries incorrectly, or modifying the original cuts array. Real code pattern uses sorted() and list concatenation.

examples.trace:
1. Original cuts: [1,3,4,5]
2. Sort cuts: already sorted -> [1,3,4,5]
3. Add boundary 0 at beginning: [0,1,3,4,5]
4. Add boundary n=7 at end: [0,1,3,4,5,7]
5. Result: [0,1,3,4,5,7]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Segment Length Between Boundaries
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation: calculate the length of a stick segment given two boundary positions.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the length of a stick segment between two boundary positions. Given two numbers a and b representing positions on the stick (with a < b), the segment length is simply b - a. This is fundamental because the cost of making a cut equals the length of the segment being cut at that moment. In the context of the problem, boundaries are stored in a sorted array 'cuts', so for indices i and j, the segment length is cuts[j] - cuts[i]. The intuition is straightforward: physical length is difference in positions. This operation is performed repeatedly in the recurrence (for each potential cut k, we compute cuts[j]-cuts[i] as the cost). It's an O(1) time operation with O(1) space. This connects to L3 as it's used on the prepared boundaries array, and to L5 which involves basic arithmetic. Common mistakes include subtracting in wrong order (should be larger minus smaller), using wrong indices, or not ensuring a < b. Real code pattern is a simple subtraction.

examples.trace:
1. cuts[0]=0, cuts[3]=9
2. Segment length = cuts[3] - cuts[0] = 9 - 0 = 9
3. Output: 9

1. cuts[1]=1, cuts[2]=4
2. Segment length = 4 - 1 = 3
3. Output: 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Array Indexing and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Prerequisite: accessing array elements and performing subtraction.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves two basic skills: (1) Accessing elements in an array using indices, and (2) Performing subtraction arithmetic. For array indexing, given an array arr and an integer index i, arr[i] retrieves the element at position i (0-based indexing). It's essential to ensure i is within bounds (0 <= i < len(arr)). For subtraction, given two numbers a and b, a - b computes their difference. In this problem context, we use these skills together: from a sorted array of boundary positions, we access two elements at indices i and j, then subtract the smaller from the larger to get segment length. The intuition is that arrays store ordered data, and subtraction finds distances. This is O(1) time and space. This connects to L4 as the building block for computing segment lengths. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors, wrong order of subtraction leading to negative lengths, or confusion between 0-based and 1-based indexing. Real code pattern uses square brackets for indexing and the minus operator.

examples.trace:
1. Array arr = [10,20,30,40]
2. Access arr[1] = 20
3. Access arr[3] = 40
4. Subtract: arr[3] - arr[1] = 40 - 20 = 20
5. Output: 20

1. Array arr = [5,8,12]
2. Access arr[0] = 5
3. Access arr[2] = 12
4. Subtract: 12 - 5 = 7
5. Output: 7

approaches.name: Approach 3: Optimized DP with Knuth Optimization
approaches.summary: Apply Knuth optimization to the interval DP approach, reducing time complexity by exploiting the quadrangle inequality property. This optimization applies when the cost function satisfies certain monotonicity conditions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Cost to Cut a Stick with Knuth Optimization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using Knuth-optimized DP to minimize cutting cost
ladders.explanation: This is the full problem solution using Knuth optimization on interval DP. The intuition is that cutting a stick at given positions has optimal substructure: the minimum cost to cut a segment equals the segment length plus the sum of minimum costs for left and right subsegments. Knuth optimization applies when the optimal partition point k for interval [i,j] satisfies opt[i][j-1] ≤ opt[i][j] ≤ opt[i+1][j], allowing us to reduce the search space from O(m) to O(1) per state. The algorithm first sorts cuts and adds endpoints 0 and n. We define dp[i][j] as minimum cost to cut between cuts[i] and cuts[j], with opt[i][j] storing the optimal k for dp[i][j]. Initialize dp[i][i+1] = 0 for adjacent cuts. For increasing lengths, compute dp[i][j] = min_{k from opt[i][j-1] to opt[i+1][j]} (dp[i][k] + dp[k][j]) + (cuts[j]-cuts[i]). Time is O(m²) since each state computes in O(1) instead of O(m). Space is O(m²) for dp and opt tables. This connects to L1 by implementing the full Knuth optimization loop structure. Common mistakes include forgetting to sort cuts, incorrect bounds for k search, or mis-handling base cases. Real code pattern: sort cuts, add endpoints, initialize dp and opt, fill dp for increasing lengths using Knuth bounds.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Knuth Optimization DP Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP filling with Knuth's monotonicity bounds
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the dynamic programming loop with Knuth optimization for interval DP problems. The intuition is that for many interval DP problems where the cost function satisfies the quadrangle inequality, the optimal partition point k for interval [i,j] is monotonic: opt[i][j-1] ≤ opt[i][j] ≤ opt[i+1][j]. This allows us to search only within these bounds instead of all k from i+1 to j-1, reducing time from O(m³) to O(m²). The algorithm takes sorted cuts array with endpoints, initializes dp[i][i+1]=0 and opt[i][i+1]=i. For length L from 2 to m-1 (where m is number of cuts including endpoints), for i from 0 to m-L-1, j=i+L, set left=opt[i][j-1], right=opt[i+1][j], compute dp[i][j]=min_{k from left to right} (dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]) + (cuts[j]-cuts[i]), updating opt[i][j] to the best k. Time is O(m²) because each state computes in O(right-left+1) which amortizes to O(1). Space is O(m²) for dp and opt. This connects to L0 as the main computational engine. Common mistakes include incorrect bounds initialization, off-by-one errors in loop ranges, or not handling cases where left>right. Real code pattern: for length in range(2, m): for i in range(m-length): j=i+length; left=opt[i][j-1]; right=opt[i+1][j]; best=INF; for k in range(left, right+1): val=dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]; if val<best: best=val; opt[i][j]=k; dp[i][j]=best+(cuts[j]-cuts[i]).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Interval DP with Optimal Partition
ladders.desc: Dynamic programming over intervals to find minimum cutting cost
ladders.explanation: This core logic solves the minimum stick cutting problem using interval dynamic programming without optimization. The intuition is that cutting a stick can be broken into subproblems: to cut between positions cuts[i] and cuts[j], we choose a cut point k between them, pay cost (cuts[j]-cuts[i]) for the current cut, and add costs for left and right segments. This exhibits optimal substructure, allowing DP. Define dp[i][j] as minimum cost to cut all cuts between cuts[i] and cuts[j], where cuts includes endpoints 0 and n. Base case: dp[i][i+1]=0 (no cuts between adjacent points). Recurrence: dp[i][j] = min_{i<k<j} (dp[i][k] + dp[k][j]) + (cuts[j]-cuts[i]). We fill dp for increasing interval lengths. Time is O(m³) from triple nested loops, space O(m²). This connects to L1 as the unoptimized version that Knuth improves. Common mistakes include not sorting cuts, incorrect indices in recurrence, or missing base cases. Real code pattern: sort cuts, add 0 and n, m=len(cuts), dp=[[0]*m for _ in range(m)]; for length in range(2, m): for i in range(m-length): j=i+length; dp[i][j]=INF; for k in range(i+1, j): dp[i][j]=min(dp[i][j], dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]); dp[i][j]+=cuts[j]-cuts[i]; return dp[0][m-1].

examples.trace: m=3. Initialize dp[0][1]=0, dp[1][2]=0. Compute dp[0][2]: i=0, j=2, length=2. k from 1 to 1: k=1: dp[0][1]+dp[1][2]=0+0=0. Add cost cuts[2]-cuts[0]=5-0=5, so dp[0][2]=5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Cutting Stick Cost Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute the cost of cutting a stick segment at given points
ladders.explanation: This building block calculates the total cost of cutting a stick segment between two endpoints at specified cut points. The intuition is that each cut costs the length of the current stick segment being cut. Given a stick from position a to b and a list of cut positions between them, if we cut at position c, we pay (b-a) for that cut, then recursively cut left segment [a,c] and right segment [c,b]. This recursive definition leads to the total cost being sum of lengths of all segments created during cutting. For example, cutting a stick of length 10 at positions 3 and 7: first cut at 3 costs 10, creating segments [0,3] and [3,10]; then cut [3,10] at 7 costs 7, total 17. The algorithm can be implemented recursively: function cost(a,b,cuts): if no cuts between a and b, return 0; else total=0; for each cut c between a and b: total += (b-a) + cost(a,c,cuts_left) + cost(c,b,cuts_right). Time is exponential O(2^m) in naive recursion, space O(m) for recursion stack. This connects to L2 as the recursive formulation that DP optimizes. Common mistakes include double-counting costs or incorrect base case. Real code pattern: def cost(a,b,cuts): if not cuts: return 0; total=INF; for c in cuts: left=[x for x in cuts if x<c]; right=[x for x in cuts if x>c]; total=min(total, (b-a)+cost(a,c,left)+cost(c,b,right)); return total.

examples.trace: First, cuts between 0 and 10 are [3,7]. Try cut at 3: cost=10 + cost(0,3,[]) + cost(3,10,[7]). cost(0,3,[])=0. cost(3,10,[7]): cut at 7: cost=7 + cost(3,7,[]) + cost(7,10,[])=7+0+0=7. Total=10+0+7=17. Try cut at 7: cost=10 + cost(0,7,[3]) + cost(7,10,[]). cost(0,7,[3]): cut at 3: cost=7 + cost(0,3,[]) + cost(3,7,[])=7+0+0=7. Total=10+7+0=17. Minimum is 17.

Cuts between 0 and 7 are [1,3,4,5]. Try cut at 1: cost=7 + cost(0,1,[]) + cost(1,7,[3,4,5])=7+0+cost(1,7,[3,4,5]). cost(1,7,[3,4,5]): try cut at 3: cost=6 + cost(1,3,[]) + cost(3,7,[4,5])=6+0+cost(3,7,[4,5]). cost(3,7,[4,5]): try cut at 4: cost=4 + cost(3,4,[]) + cost(4,7,[5])=4+0+cost(4,7,[5]). cost(4,7,[5]): cut at 5: cost=3 + cost(4,5,[]) + cost(5,7,[])=3+0+0=3. So cost(3,7,[4,5])=4+0+3=7. Then cost(1,7,[3,4,5])=6+0+7=13. Total=7+0+13=20. Other cuts yield similar; optimal is 16 as in full problem.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Segment Length Addition
ladders.desc: Add up lengths of stick segments during cutting
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves summing the lengths of stick segments created when making cuts. The intuition is that each time you cut a stick, the cost is the current segment's length, and this length gets added to the total cost. For example, a stick of length L cut into two segments of lengths L1 and L2: the cut costs L, and if you further cut those segments, their lengths add to the total. The total cost equals the sum of lengths of all initial segments that undergo cutting. Mathematically, if you have a stick from a to b and cut at points c1,c2,...,ck in order, total cost = (b-a) + sum over each subsegment created. The algorithm is straightforward: given endpoints a and b and cut points sorted between them, total=0; current_start=a; for each cut c in sorted cuts: total += (c-current_start); current_start=c; total += (b-current_start) for the final segment? Wait, careful: actually, the cost is not sum of segment lengths, but sum of lengths of segments being cut. For cutting, each cut adds the length of the segment at time of cut. So to compute total, simulate cuts: start with segment [a,b], cost=0; while cuts not empty: choose a cut c, cost += (b-a); split into [a,c] and [c,b]; recursively add costs. But as a basic operation, we practice adding lengths: e.g., stick length 10, cut at 3 and 7: first cut adds 10, second cut adds 7, total 17. Time O(m) to sum, space O(1). This connects to L3 as the arithmetic at each recursive step. Common mistakes include adding wrong lengths or misunderstanding cost accumulation. Real code pattern: total=0; for cut in cuts: total += (cut - a) or similar based on simulation; but simpler: given order of cuts, total = sum of (current segment length) for each cut.

examples.trace: Start with segment [0,10], length 10. Cut at 3: add 10 to total, total=10. Now segments [0,3] and [3,10]. Next cut at 7 is in [3,10], length 7. Add 7 to total, total=17. No more cuts, total=17.

Order matters; assume optimal order. Start [0,7], length 7, cut at 3: add 7, total=7. Segments [0,3] and [3,7]. Cut [3,7] at 4: length 4, add 4, total=11. Segments [3,4] and [4,7]. Cut [4,7] at 5: length 3, add 3, total=14. Cut [0,3] at 1: length 3, add 3, total=17? That gives 17, but optimal is 16. So order affects total; this shows why we need DP to find min. For basic op, we just compute given order.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stick and Cut Points
ladders.desc: Understand the problem setup: a stick with marked cut positions
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces the basic elements of the problem: a stick of length n, represented as a line segment from 0 to n, and a list of cut positions between 0 and n where we need to make cuts. The intuition is that we want to cut the stick at all given positions, and each cut costs the length of the current piece being cut. After a cut, the stick splits into two pieces, and we continue cutting on each piece if needed. The goal is to minimize total cost by choosing the order of cuts. For example, with stick length 10 and cut at position 3: we must cut at 3, costing 10, resulting in pieces of lengths 3 and 7. If another cut at 7, we cut the piece [3,10] at 7, costing 7, total 17. The key is that cut order matters: cutting at 7 first then 3 might give different cost. This is a combinatorial optimization problem. The algorithm at this level is just problem understanding: input n and cuts list, output minimum cost. Time and space are not applicable yet. This connects to L4 as the scenario where we add costs. Common mistakes include thinking cuts can be outside 0-n or that cost is per cut regardless of length. Real code pattern: just problem statement: given n and cuts, find min cost.

examples.trace: Stick from 0 to 7. Need to cut at positions 3 and 5. Possible orders: cut at 3 first: cost 7, pieces [0,3] and [3,7]. Then cut [3,7] at 5: cost 4, total 11. Cut at 5 first: cost 7, pieces [0,5] and [5,7]. Then cut [0,5] at 3: cost 5, total 12. So min cost is 11.

Stick from 0 to 10. Cut at 2 and 8. Order 2 then 8: cut at 2 costs 10, pieces [0,2] and [2,10]. Cut [2,10] at 8 costs 8, total 18. Order 8 then 2: cut at 8 costs 10, pieces [0,8] and [8,10]. Cut [0,8] at 2 costs 8, total 18. Same cost, min 18.
